coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid and octadecylamine co-derived carbon dots for multi-mode high performance liquid chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jin-Cheng
Gao, Shuo
Zhang, Jing-Han
Lv, Hai-Tao
Wu, Qi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel kind of carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized by citric acid, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid and octadecylamine. Due to the variety of precursors, the synthesized CDs possess multiple functional groups including carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl and imidazole groups. One CDs functionalized silica gels (Si-CDs) chromatographic column can be applied in three separation modes including reversed-phase liquid chromatography, hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography and ion exchange chromatography, suitable for separating compounds with different properties. Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, alkylbenzenes, alkaloids, pesticides, antibiotics, sulfonamides, glycosides, inorganic anions, aromatic acids, nucleosides and bases as test compounds can be separated well on this Si-CDs column. For four adjacent analytes of triadimefon, tebuconazole, diniconazole and diclofop, the resolution reaches 3.77, 2.34 and 5.17 with an average column efficiency of 9500 plates/m. Retention mechanism contains hydrophobic, π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions according to linear solvation energy relationship. This Si-CDs column is used for real sample analysis of residual thiamphenicol and chloramphenicol in milk. With simplified sample pretreatment process, spiked recoveries between 89.3%-112% indicates the developed detection method based on this Si-CDs column is not only rapid but also reliable. Using CDs with multiple functional ligands as modifiers for multi-mode stationary phases avoids multi-step modification process of various small molecules on silica gels, which is helpful to the evolution of multi-mode chromatography and nanomaterials-based separation science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stationary phase
Carbon dots
Multi-mode chromatography
Retention mechanism
HPLC

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of CDs and Si-CDs stationary phase
Chromatographic performance of Si-CDs stationary phase

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087), the High-Level Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis diagram of Si-CDs stationary phases (the structure of CDs is only hypothetical, does not represent their real structure).
(a) TEM image of CDs; (b) XRD spectrum of CDs; (c) FTIR spectra of CDs and Si-CDs; (d) LSCM image of Si-CDs.

Chromatograms of alkylbenzenes (a), PAHs (b), alkaloids (c), pesticides (d), glycosides (e), antibiotics (f) on Si-CDs column. Analytes: (a) (1) benzene, (2) toluene, (3) ethylbenzene, (4) propylbenzene, (5) butylbenzene, (6) phenylpentane; (b) (1) o-phenanthroline, (2) naphthalene, (3) biphenyl, (4) fluorene, (5) phenanthrene, (6) fluoranthene (7) 1,3-diphenylbenzene; (c) (1) theobromine, (2) caffeine, (3) theophylline; (d) (1) metalaxyl, (2) triadimefon, (3) tebuconazole, (4) diniconazole, (5) diclofop; (e) (1) salicin, (2) baicalin, (3) hesperidin; (f) (1) thiamphenicol, (2) pazufloxacin, (3) chloramphenicol, (4) ciprofloxacin; Mobile phase: (a) MeOH/H2O (55/45), (b) MeOH/H2O (60/40), (c) MeOH/H2O (30/70), (d) MeOH/H2O (50/50), (e) MeOH/H2O (50/50), (f) MeOH/20 mM ammonium acetate (30/70); Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1.
Plots of experimental log k versus calculated log k in LSER.
Chromatograms of milk extracts without and with added CAP and TAP; Mobile phase: 10% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate; Detection wavelength: 240 nm.
Chromatographic parameters under different separation modes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: k: retention factor, k = (tR-t0)/t0, tR: retention time, t0: dead time

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: N: number of theoretical plates, N=5.54(tR/W1/2)2, W1/2: peak width at half height

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: As : asymmetry factor, As = d2/d1, d1: the distance from the summit to the leading edge of peak at 10% height, d2: the distance from the summit to the trailing edge of peak at 10% height

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Rs : resolution, Rs = 2(tR2-tR1)/(W1+W2), W: peak width at base line

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid and octadecylamine co-derived carbon dots for multi-mode high performance liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel kind of carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized by citric acid, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid and octadecylamine. Due to the variety of precursors, the synthesized CDs possess multiple functional groups including carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl and imidazole groups. One CDs functionalized silica gels (Si-CDs) chromatographic column can be applied in three separation modes including reversed-phase liquid chromatography, hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography and ion exchange chromatography, suitable for separating compounds with different properties. Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, alkylbenzenes, alkaloids, pesticides, antibiotics, sulfonamides, glycosides, inorganic anions, aromatic acids, nucleosides and bases as test compounds can be separated well on this Si-CDs column. For four adjacent analytes of triadimefon, tebuconazole, diniconazole and diclofop, the resolution reaches 3.77, 2.34 and 5.17 with an average column efficiency of 9500 plates/m. Retention mechanism contains hydrophobic, π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions according to linear solvation energy relationship. This Si-CDs column is used for real sample analysis of residual thiamphenicol and chloramphenicol in milk. With simplified sample pretreatment process, spiked recoveries between 89.3%-112% indicates the developed detection method based on this Si-CDs column is not only rapid but also reliable. Using CDs with multiple functional ligands as modifiers for multi-mode stationary phases avoids multi-step modification process of various small molecules on silica gels, which is helpful to the evolution of multi-mode chromatography and nanomaterials-based separation science.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) has been widely applied in environmental analysis, food analysis, pharmaceutical analysis, etc. It is a goal for scientists engaged in chromatography that one chromatographic column can be used for separating various kinds of compounds [1,2]. Multi-mode chromatography can be applied in several separation modes such as normal phase liquid chromatography, reversed phase liquid chromatography (RPLC), ion exchange chromatography (IEC) and hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC), has been a hot topic in separation science [3–5]. In general, multi-mode chromatographic stationary phases require a variety of functional groups with different properties to achieve multiple retention mechanisms [6–8]. Thus, multifunctional materials are crucial for the development of multi-mode chromatography [9–11].

Carbon dots (CDs) with low toxicity, good dispersibility, excellent photoluminescence stability, have been favored in many fields such as imaging, sensing, anti-counterfeiting and encryption [12–14]. CDs derived from various kinds of precursors have been used as functional ligands for silica gels-based stationary phases. Surprisingly, CDs-decorated silica stationary phases presented good separation performance, which highlighted the advantages of nanomaterials in chromatographic separation [15]. However, most of the synthesized CDs can only be applied in single separation mode such as HILIC [16–20] or RPLC [21,22]. To the best of our knowledge, CDs have been scarcely applied in multi-mode chromatography. Recently, octadecylamine and citric acid co-derived CDs are synthesized and decorated on silica gels. This CDs-modified silica stationary phase is applied successfully in RPLC and HILIC [23], which opens up a new way for CDs in HPLC. One-step synthesis of CDs with multiple functional groups and proper hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity as functional ligands for stationary phases, not only avoids complex modification process of many kinds of small molecules on stationary phases, but also makes full use of the advantages of nanomaterials. Thus, more functional CDs are needed to promote the development and innovation of HPLC.

In this work, novel CDs are synthesized using citric acid, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid and octadecylamine as precursors. The separation performance of CDs-decorated silica stationary phase (Si-CDs) under RPLC, HILIC and IEC modes is explored using many kinds of compounds as probes. The retention mechanism is investigated by linear solvation energy relationship (LSER). The repeatability and reproducibility for preparing the Si-CDs columns are tested and the column stability is also evaluated. The applicability of the Si-CDs column for real sample analysis is tested by detecting residual thiamphenicol (TAP) and chloramphenicol (CAP) in milk as an example. CAP and TAP belong to the same class of antibiotics, their chemical structures are similar. They are widely used in the treatment of bovine pneumonia, mastitis and arthritis. The residual antibiotics and corresponding products do great harm to human health through bioaccumulation. Here, a simple and accurate method for simultaneous detection of CAP and TAP is established based on this Si-CDs column.

Citric acid, octadecylamine, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and standard chemicals are provided from Meryer Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Porous silica gels (100 Å, 5 μm) are purchased from Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd (Japan). 1-Aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid is provided from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, CAS (China). Methanol (MeOH) and acetonitrile (ACN) in HPLC grade are purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Milk is bought from local supermarket (RT Mart, Qingdao, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) characterization is performed on a JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray crystal diffraction (XRD) characterization is carried on D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (BrukerAXS, Germany). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging is carried out on a FLUOVIEW FV1200 biological confocal laser scanning microscope (OLYMPUS, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) test is acquired on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 instrument (Thermo, USA). Surface area and pore size analysis are performed on a NOVA 2200e instrument (Quantachrome, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis is carried on a METTLER TOLEDO TGA2 instrument (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland). Chromatographic column evaluation is carried on a Thermo UltiMate 3000 HPLC system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Inspired from our previous work, it should be necessary to control and adjust the hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity of CDs if they are aimed to be applied in multi-mode HPLC. Precursor is the key factor affecting the properties of CDs. Citric acid is the most commonly used precursor for synthesizing CDs, it is easily to form carbon core. However, CDs synthesized with citric acid are highly hydrophilic. To enhance the hydrophobicity of CDs, octadecanamine is chosen as one of the precursors. To give CDs ion exchange ability, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid is selected as one of the precursors. Through adjusting the ratio of citric acid, octadecanamine and 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid during the synthesis process, CDs with proper hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity can be obtained. The optimized synthesis procedure is as follows. First, 1.0 g citric acid, 0.3 g octadecanamine and 0.2 g 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid are dissolved in 15 mL mixed solvent of ethanol and water (v/v 2:1) with the help of stir and heat at 60℃. Then, the solution is heated at 180℃ for 4 h in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The brown CDs solution is dialyzed for 3 days to remove unreacted precursor molecules. Finally, CDs are collected after freeze drying.

The Si-CDs stationary phase is prepared by chemical modification of CDs onto silica gels with3-aminopropyltriethoxysilaneas spacer, the schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 1 . The preparation method of amino-modified silica gels refers to previous literature [24]. The dispersion system containing 3.0 g porous silica gels and 0.5 mL 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane in anhydrous toluene are heated at 100℃ for 24 h under stirring. After cooling to room temperature, the amino-modified silica particles are washed by toluene and ethanol for three times, and are dried in vacuum oven. CDs are bonded onto amino-modified silica particles through the acylation reaction of carboxyl groups in CDs and amino groups in silica gels using EDC and NHS as coupling agent. The detailed process is as follows. First, 0.2 g CDs are dispersed into the mixed solvent of ethanol and water (v/v 2:1) followed by adding 0.02 g EDC and 0.02 g NHS to active the carboxyl groups of CDs. After stirring for 30 min, 2.5 g amino-modified silica particles are added to above dispersion system and continuously stirred for 24 h at 30℃. After the reaction, the Si-CDs particles are washed by water and methanol alternately to remove the nanoparticles adsorbed on the surface of Si-CDs particles. Finally, the Si-CDs particles are dried in a vacuum oven at 60℃ for 12 h.

Si-CDs particles are dispersed in equal volume of 1,4-dioxane and carbon tetrachloride by ultrasonication, and are filled into stainless steel tubes (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.) using a HASKEL pneumatic liquid booster pump at 40 MPa with methanol as propulsion solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of CDs and Si-CDs stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: From the TEM image (Fig. 2 a), it can be seen that CDs are monodispersed spherical particles. The size statistical distribution is acquired by measuring one hundred of CDs in different visual fields, the average size of CDs is 3.2 nm. From the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig.2b), a broad diffraction peak centered at 22.6° with lattice spacing of 0.394 nm can be seen. This peak can be attributed to the (002) facet of graphite [25], which indicates the synthesized CDs have typical graphitic structure.

FTIR images of CDs and Si-CDs are shown in Fig. 2c, it can be seen a broad peak located at 3407 cm−1, which is derived from N-H and O-H stretching vibration. The absorption peaks located at 2918 cm−1 and 2848 cm−1 are derived from the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of C-H in alkyl chain. The absorption peaks located at 1704 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of C=O in COOH and HNCO, respectively. The above characteristic peaks reveal the existence of amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and alkyl groups in CDs. These carbon-, nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups in CDs may be derived from polymerized precursors which have not been completely carbonized. The above characteristic peaks also occur in Si-CDs stationary phase, which verifies the effective modification of CDs on silica gels. Considering the fluorescence properties of CDs, the modification of CDs is further verified by LSCM. It can be seen bright blue fluorescence emitted from Si-CDs as shown in Fig. 2d, which demonstrates the existence of CDs on silica gels.

The specific surface area of Si-CDs stationary phase is tested through N2 adsorption and desorption experiments. Experimental results show the specific surface area increase from 287.00 to 331.08 m2/g after modification of CDs on silica gels. Thermogravimetric analysis is used to test the thermostability of stationary phase and the loading content of CDs in stationary phase. Observing the thermogravimetric curves of Si-CDs in Fig. S1, there is no significant weight loss when the temperature below 250℃, showing its good thermostability. When the temperature exceeds 250℃, the weight starts to slowly reduce. Until the temperature exceeds 400℃, we see significant weight reduction. The weight loss occurs at high temperature can be due to the decomposition of CDs since the high thermostability of silica gels. Comparing the final weight loss of Si-NH2 and Si-CDs, it can be calculated that the loading amount of CDs in stationary phase is about 3%, in other words, the bonding amount of CDs on silica gels can be roughly estimated as 30mg/g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic performance of Si-CDs stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: The retention behavior of the Si-CDs column under different separation modes is studied by exploring the change trend of retention capacity with the contents of organic phase in mobile phase. The change trend of retention capacity in the range of 40-80% MeOH contents is shown in Fig. S4 a, the log k values of hydrophobic compounds decrease gradually with the increased concentration of MeOH in mobile phase, which is in accord with the retention characteristic of RPLC [26]. While under HILIC mode, the retention behavior is opposite to that of RPLC. As shown in Fig. S4b, in the range of 60-90% ACN contents, the log k values of amino acids increase gradually with the increased concentration of ACN in mobile phase, showing a typical retention characteristic of HILIC [27]. Generally, the ion exchange performance is mainly influenced by the ion strength or salt concentration of eluents which affects the ion pair interaction between stationary phase and solutes, as well as pH of mobile phase which affects the surface charge status of solutes. Thus, the change trend of retention capacity with the salt concentration and pH is explored under IEC mode. As shown in Fig. S4c, the log k values of inorganic anions decrease gradually with the increased salt concentration in mobile phases, this is because that the presence of salt in eluent will restrain or weaken the electrostatic attraction interaction between stationary phase and anions due to the competitive adsorption of salts. See in Fig. S4d, the log k values of sulfonamides increase obviously with the increase of mobile phase pH. This is reasonable since with the increase of pH, the protonated NH2 in sulfonamides reduces and the electrostatic repulsive force between sulfonamides and CDs decreases. Above phenomena confirm the mixed retention mechanism of this Si-CDs column.

The retention mechanism of this Si-CDs column is deeply researched by LSER which has been widely employed to investigate the structure-retention dependencies of analytes on stationary phases [28]. In brief, the theory of LSER can be condensed into a formula listed below, which is improved by Chirita et al. [29] based on original Abraham equation [30].
log k = e E
+ s S
+ a A
+ b B
+ v V
+
d -
D -
+
d +
D +
+ c

In this equation, capital letters represent the solute descriptors, lower case letters represent the system constants. E is the excess molar refraction (calculated from the refractive index of the molecule) and models polarizability contributions from n and π electrons. S is the solute dipolarity/polarizability. A and B are the overall hydrogen-bond acidity and basicity of solute. V is the McGowan characteristic volume. D− and D+ represents the negative charge and the positive charge carried by anionic and zwitterionic species, respectively. c is the model intercept term, is not assigned any chemical significance. k is the retention factor of analytes on stationary phases. The system constants (e, s, a, b, d −, d+ and v) reflect the magnitude of difference for that particular property between the mobile phases and stationary phases.

To construct the LSER model, we selected 32 standards including hydrophobic, polar, hydrophilic and ionizable compounds as the model compounds. Solute descriptors (E, S, A, B, V) of the 32 model compounds are consulted from references [29,31,32], D− and D+ are calculated based on the equations in reference [29] according to the real pH of mobile phase. The log k under mobile phase condition of MeOH/20 mM ammonium acetate (40%/60%, v/v) are recorded. The reason for choosing this mobile phase condition is that all model compounds can obtain appropriate retention under this condition. Above data can be consulted in Table S1. Through a multilinear regression of the retention data and various property parameters of solutes, the LSER equation for the developed Si-CDs stationary phase can be obtained as below.

In this equation, the magnitude of the system coefficient reflects the contribution of some interactions to the retention, which can encode chromatographic system properties. A positive system coefficient indicates stronger interaction of solutes on stationary phase not in mobile phase. While a negative system coefficient demonstrates that solutes prefer to stay in the mobile phase or weak retention ability of stationary phase.

According to the definition of solute descriptors, the e system coefficient reflects the π-π interaction between stationary phase or mobile phase and solutes. In this chromatographic system, a positive e system coefficient is obtained, which demonstrates that solutes with large π electronic system (having a positive E coefficient) will get retention on stationary phase. In other words, π-π interaction contributes on retention. The retention ability of this Si-CDs column through π-π interaction can be derived from the aromatic skeleton and the residual imidazole groups in CDs.

The s coefficient reflects the interaction between stationary phase or mobile phase and dipolar and/or polarizable solutes. A negative s value is obtained in this case, indicating that polar solutes prefer to stay in mobile phase. That is to say this Si-CDs column has weak retention ability towards polarizable solutes. Under RPLC condition, the mobile phase containing high percentage of ammonium acetate aqueous solution has stronger polarity than the stationary phase, it is not difficult to understand that solutes will be more distributed in mobile phase through strong dipole-dipole interaction.

The a and b coefficients both present the contribution of hydrogen-bond interaction on retention. According to the principle of complementarity, the a coefficient denotes the hydrogen-bond basicity or hydrogen-bond acceptor property of stationary phase or mobile phase, since solutes with hydrogen-bond acidity (having a positive A coefficient) will interact with basic phases. Similarly, the b coefficient denotes the hydrogen-bond acidity or hydrogen-bond donor property of the phases. Generally, a and b coefficients are negative values under RPLC system especially for b who is always large and negative values [33,34]. In this case, a large and negative b coefficient is also obtained, meaning the strong hydrogen bonding interaction between mobile phase and solutes with hydrogen-bond basicity. The strong hydrogen-bond acidity of mobile phase originates from the Lewis acidic character of water. Though the a coefficient in this chromatographic system is still a negative value, it is larger than that obtained from conventional RP chromatographic columns [35]. This indicates that solutes with hydrogen-bond acidity will get more effective retention on the Si-CDs column than traditional C18 column. The retention ability of this Si-CDs stationary phase through hydrogen-bond interaction benefits from the oxygen-containing and nitrogenous groups in CDs.

The v coefficient represents the combined interaction of cavity formation and dispersion forces, reflecting the hydrophobicity of the chromatographic system [34]. A large and positive v coefficient is obtained in this system, which means hydrophobic interaction plays a key role on retention. The hydrophobicity of stationary phase can be derived from the alkyl chains in CDs. This conclusion is in accord with the real retention behaviour that analytes with stronger hydrophobicity have stronger retention on this Si-CDs column.

The d coefficients correspond to complementary interaction with charged compounds, which can be simply understood as electrostatic interaction. In the current situation, a large and positive d − coefficient is obtained, meaning that the existence of a negative charge in the solute structure will lead to increased retention. While a negative and large d + coefficient is obtained, demonstrating that carrying a positive charge will deduces the retention of solutes on stationary phase. In other words, the stationary phase exhibits a positively charged property, and possesses electrostatic attraction interaction with negatively charged solutes, and electrostatic repulsion interaction with positively charged solutes. This conclusion is consistent with the structural features of CDs with imidazolium cation and real retention behaviour of this Si-CDs stationary phase.

To sum up, the retention mechanism of this Si-CDs column under RPLC mode contains hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions. In order to further test the reliability of the LSER model, we studied the consistency between the experimental retention data and the calculated retention data based on obtained LSER equation. As shown in Fig. 4, the black line denotes the ideal case in which the experimental and calculated log K values are equal, the red line is the fitted curve between the calculated log k and the experimental log k. The equation for the fitted curve is y = 0.9454x + 0.0275 (R2 = 0.9249), from which we see the calculated log k is very close to the experimental log k. Regular residuals with absolute values higher than 0.25 can be considered outliers, only three outliers are detected in this data set, which means that the conclusion drawn from the constructed LSER model is credible.

To verify the practicability of the home-made Si-CDs column, it is used for detecting residual antibiotics in milk. The conventional HPLC method for determination of CAP and TAP in milk requires a series of complex sample pretreatment processes, including extraction, degrease and solid phase extraction column purification [36,37], which is accurate but not conducive to rapid detection. Here, a simpler sample pretreatment method is proposed. In brief, the milk is added with acetonitrile to extract residual CAP and TAP, the precipitate and solution are separated by centrifugation, the supernatant is further filtered through a nylon filter membrane (0.22 μm) before inject to HPLC for detection. CAP and TAP are not detected in the milk. To verify the accuracy of this method, standard addition and recovery experiments are carried out. The chromatograms of milk samples with and without added antibiotics are shown in Fig. 5 , CAP and TAP can be separated well from interfering substances under isocratic elution within 15 min. The recovery of spiked standard is between 89.3%∼112% (Table S2), in the allowable range of error. The standard curves for determining the concentrations of TAP and CAP in the range of 1-20 μg/L are shown in Fig S5, the linear equation for determining CAP is A=9008c+3790 (R2=0.989), the LOD (3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation of the blank and k is the slope of the calibration plot) is 0.83 μg/L. The linear equation for determining TAP is A=8825c+2947 (R2=0.989), the LOD is 0.88 μg/L, which is lower than the maximum residue limit regulated by European Union. Above experimental results indicate the practicability of this Si-CDs column and the detection method is not only rapid but also accurate.

The intra-day and inter-day repeatability, as well as inter-batch reproducibility of Si-CDs columns are tested, the reproducibility results are shown in Fig. S6. The RSD of retention time for run-to-run repeatability (n=10) is lower than 3.1%, the RSD of retention time for daily repeatability (n=3) is lower than 3.7%, and the RSD of retention time for inter-batch reproducibility (n=3) is lower than 8.9%. Though the repeatability and reproducibility are in a reasonable range, they are not as good as small molecule modified columns. This gives us a wake-up call that the heterogeneity including size and surface state difference of CDs should be an obstacle for commercialization of CDs-based chromatographic columns.

The inorganic anions selected as test analytes are separated repeatedly as time goes on, the retention time and N have no obvious change after 60 h (Fig. S7). Actually, this Si-CDs column has been used about 5 months atirregularintervals in our lab, no obvious degeneration of column performance is found, meaning the developed column meets routine laboratory requirement. Since the pH tolerance range of silica gels is between 2.0-8.0, the tolerance of this Si-CDs column under acidic (pH 2.5) and alkaline (pH 7.5) environment is tested. As shown in Fig. S8, after long-term flushing by acidic and alkaline mobile phases, the separation performance does not degrade, indicating the good stability of this Si-CDs column.

A novel kind of CDs is synthesized using citric acid, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ionic liquid and octadecylamine as precursors. The synthesized CDs have functional groups of alkyl chain, imidazole group, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The Si-CDs stationary phase presents good separation performance under RPLC, HILIC and IEC modes. Various kinds of analytes including PAHs, alkylbenzenes, alkaloids, pesticides, antibiotics, sulfonamides, glycosides, inorganic anions, aromatic acids, nucleosides and bases are separated well on this Si-CDs column. The retention mechanism is explored by LSER, from which we know that hydrophobic, π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions play important roles on retention. The Si-CDs column is applied in real sample analysis for detecting residual TAP and CAP in milk with simplified sample pretreatment process. In this work, by selecting hydrophilic and hydrophobic precursors, CDs that can be used in multiple chromatographic separation modes are synthesized, which expands the types of separable and detectable compounds on a single column, and helps to promote the development of separation science.

Jin-Cheng Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shuo Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Jing-Han Zhang: Validation. Hai-Tao Lv: Supervision. Qi Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087), the High-Level Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107932.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in mixed-mode chromatographic stationary phases
J. Sep. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Sykora, P. Rezanka, K. Zaruba, V. Kral, Recent advances in mixed-mode chromatographic stationary phases, J. Sep. Sci. 42 (2019) 89-129. 10.1002/jssc.201801048

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mixed mode chromatography complex development for large opportunities
Curr. Protein Pept. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Cabanne, X. Santarelli, Mixed mode chromatography complex development for large opportunities, Curr. Protein Pept. Sci. 20 (2019) 22-27. 10.2174/1389203718666171030104211

J. Sep. Sci.
